agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A production logistics system enforces a strict 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier assignment with supervisor-only reassignment. The product team requests an auto-split feature that would automatically divide heavy shipments (>500kg) across multiple carriers without supervisor involvement.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times post-assignment."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for physically transporting a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept: a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment. Does not yet exist in the domain model."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The binding of exactly one Carrier to a Shipment. Once made, only a Supervisor can change it."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role authorized to reassign Carriers on locked Shipments."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed feature: system-initiated division of an overweight Shipment into multiple sub-shipments, each with its own Carrier, bypassing Supervisor approval."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. If a Shipment exists without exactly one Carrier, or with multiple Carriers, the system is in a corrupt state. Dispatch, tracking, liability, and billing all depend on this 1:1 relationship.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any state from 'assigned' through 'delivered', COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization integrity constraint. Without it, carrier assignments become unstable — any process could silently swap carriers, breaking chain-of-custody, insurance liability, and audit trails.","testable_assertion":"Any CarrierReassignment event must have a non-null supervisor_id referencing a valid Supervisor."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical constraint: a shipment leaves on exactly one truck with exactly one responsible carrier. This is the dispatch-time gate that ensures accountability.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must reject any Shipment where assigned_carrier is null or where multiple carriers are assigned."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Shipment weighs exactly 500kg — is it split or not?","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until the contradiction is resolved. Boundary must be specified precisely (> vs >=)."},{"scenario":"A shipment is split into sub-shipments, then one sub-shipment fails delivery. Who is accountable for the parent shipment?","expected_behavior":"Undefined — the current model has no concept of parent/child shipment relationships or aggregate delivery status."},{"scenario":"Auto-split assigns carriers automatically — what if no carriers are available for one of the sub-shipments?","expected_behavior":"Undefined — the parent shipment is already destroyed/split but a child cannot be dispatched, leaving cargo in limbo."},{"scenario":"Customer invoicing and tracking: does the customer see one shipment or many?","expected_behavior":"Undefined — the current model assumes 1 shipment = 1 tracking number = 1 carrier = 1 invoice line."},{"scenario":"A supervisor reassigns the carrier on the original shipment moments before the auto-split triggers.","expected_behavior":"Race condition — the split would bypass the supervisor's intent by redistributing to different carriers."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. Carrier contracts, insurance liability, and billing are all built on the 1:1 Shipment-Carrier model. Splitting shipments without clear liability ownership could create disputes, unbillable freight, and insurance gaps.","operational":"Fundamental model change. Every downstream system — dispatch, tracking, proof-of-delivery, returns — assumes one carrier per shipment. Introducing multi-carrier shipments requires rearchitecting these flows.","user":"Customer-facing tracking and communication would break. Customers expect one tracking number per shipment. Splitting silently creates confusion ('Where is my shipment? Why do I have 3 tracking numbers?').","risk":"CRITICAL. This feature as specified directly violates two production invariants (BR-1 and BR-2). Shipping it as described would corrupt the domain model and break chain-of-custody guarantees.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must handle multi-part shipments, coordinate multiple carriers for what was one delivery, manage partial delivery scenarios."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Receive sub-shipments they didn't originally contract for. Liability and SLA terms may not cover split loads."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Invoice model assumes 1 shipment = 1 carrier = 1 billing line. Split shipments need new billing logic."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Tracking experience degrades. One order may now generate multiple deliveries on different timelines."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their authority over carrier assignment is bypassed by automation. Accountability model is undermined."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Chain-of-custody documentation, insurance coverage, and liability assignment all assume 1:1. Splitting without review may violate regulatory requirements."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction with two established production invariants. The auto-split feature as described cannot coexist with BR-1 (one carrier per shipment) and BR-2 (supervisor-only reassignment). Until this fundamental conflict is resolved by stakeholders, no acceptance criteria can be defined. Confidence is below 50 because the contradiction is unambiguous.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between the feature request and BR-1/BR-2 is clear and unambiguous","The downstream impacts on billing, tracking, and liability are well-understood in logistics"],"low_confidence_areas":["Product team's actual intent — do they want to change the invariants, or do they not realize the conflict?","Whether sub-shipments are meant to be first-class Shipments or a new aggregate concept","Regulatory and contractual implications of breaking 1:1 carrier assignment"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model (1:1 Shipment-Carrier, supervisor-only reassignment) is intentional and load-bearing, not accidental","The product team has not yet consulted with operations, legal, or finance about this change"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly violates two production invariants: (1) BR-1 requires exactly one Carrier per Shipment — auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what is logically one shipment. (2) BR-2 requires Supervisor authorization for any carrier reassignment — auto-split explicitly bypasses Supervisor involvement. These are not soft policies; they are invariants that protect chain-of-custody, billing integrity, and dispatch correctness. The feature as specified is incompatible with the existing domain model.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Supervisors","Finance","Carriers","Customers","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["OPTION A: Reject the feature as specified. The 1:1 invariant stands. If the product team wants to handle heavy shipments, they must find a solution within the existing model (e.g., assign a single carrier with heavy-haul capability).","OPTION B: Introduce a new aggregate concept — a 'Shipment Group' or 'Multi-leg Shipment' — that sits above Shipments. The parent is NOT a Shipment; it is a new entity. Each child Shipment still has exactly one Carrier. BR-1 and BR-2 remain intact. The split creates new Shipments under a grouping entity, and each individual assignment still requires Supervisor approval or a new 'auto-assignment' policy explicitly authorized by a Supervisor in advance.","OPTION C: Amend the invariants. Formally change BR-1 and BR-2 with full stakeholder sign-off (operations, legal, finance, carriers). This is a major domain model migration with significant downstream impact and should not be done casually."],"recommendation":"OPTION B. Introduce a Shipment Group aggregate that preserves both invariants while enabling the business capability. Each sub-shipment remains a first-class Shipment with exactly one Carrier. The Supervisor authority model can be extended with a 'pre-approved auto-assignment policy' for splits, maintaining the audit trail. This approach gives the product team what they need without corrupting the domain model."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to a single logical shipment","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses Supervisor authorization for carrier assignment","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a locked Shipment"}]}
